item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Title: 1286-new.png
Author(s): Roland Turner, N/A, [[*user Faminepulse]
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by faminepulse using the images below.

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Title: Replica 'Fat Man' atom bomb, National Museum of the US Air Force, Dayton, Ohio, USA.
Author: 2018

Source: publicdomainvectors.com
License: Public Domain
Title: Lost man's face
Author: N/A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Idea? A little boring.
Dry humor? Redeems the article.
Hotel? Trivago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to upvote this one as the sentient bomb that has to be constantly talked down from blowing up is incredibly fun. Unfortunately the lack of analysis of the scip drags it down for me. I understand the argument put forward in the discussion about risk of triggering it if scanned. That said not even trying to determine its origins or understanding of its construction through conversation seems very poor. It's a Keter class scip. The Foundation has to try and improve containment beyond "Maybe it's a live A-bomb in the middle of a densely populated area but meh let's just try talking it down again and again". No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t understand how this is Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice Scip. I think it is not a bomb, just a sapient replica created by AWCY? to distract the Foundation, but it has an anomalous memetic effect that makes people think it is more dangerous than it truly is.
+1

item.title: Re: Written by?
item.content:encoded: Hey I appreciate the quick response. I try and credit the author in the video description of all of the content I upload on YouTube. Everyone should out of respect of the authors effort in my opinion. Anyhoo, Thanks S.D.

item.title: Re: Written by?
item.content:encoded: The author of this work is Faminepulse. They've got about 70 other works on the site that you can find by heading to their author page.

item.title: Written by?
item.content:encoded: The account of the writer has been deleted but I'd like to credit them anyways. Can anyone tell me who the original writer was? And thank you in advance if you can…

item.title: Link...
item.content:encoded: Your link went to a page that hasn't been created yet, but here's a working one:. https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_Star_(film)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may be out of the loop on this, but how is this AWCY related? Nothing in the article mentions it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Friendly reminder: Do not tag this as an awcy article. Related GOI is okay though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is basically Andy from RvB, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It shouldn't be able to react at all to passive scans, it doesn't go off when you look at it.

Why are we sure this is even anomalous? Something that repeats the same phrases over and over again vaguely in response to stimululi is totally plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suicidal bomb has been done before as an idea, but I like the execution so +1.

Also a point to make to the people asking "why dont they try harder to find out if it's really a bomb?"
Given the nature of SCPs even it they find it is a hollow plastic toy that doesn't guarentee it won't explode like a atomic bomb anyway.
I imagine unofficial policy is to start by assuming everything anomalous is unfairly and capriciously lethal until demonstrated otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat. The pic looks like it could be something straight out of the Fallout universe and the whole notion reminds me of an early episode of Galaxy Angel but played for terror instead of comedy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, Jenny, you cannot easily compare a fairly short text with one picture and a 1,5h long motion picture with several characters, a plot, and a completely different setting, now can you?

i like this skip. Completely mad, and completely good. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been done, and better, in 1974.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_Star_(film)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I get it. People are scared because it's not only a person threatening to detonate a nuke, it's also threatening to commit suicide, making it double scary.

And it's keter because
A) It's effect causes people to think it's dangerous, therefore keter, similarly to SCP-902
B) It's effect is mostly uncontained, and possibly dangerous, considering anxiety and heart attacks, and such, coupled with the fact that they can't stop it's threats because getting close to it causes people to freeze in fear and activate it's aforementioned effects
C) It's hard to contain. Getting close activates it's effects, and it may just activate because it wants to.

I still see AWCY written all over this thing, since it was apparently at an "art gallery"-esque thing, and was supposed to be a work of art. Maybe it's a piece of art to represent fear, and also that the fear of something may be more dangerous than the thing itself, like that, "absence of shark" SCP?

I will +1, since that origin log was pretty interesting, plus the underlying feeling that there's something going down behind the scenes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this remind anyone else of Andy the bomb from the popular webseries Red vs. Blue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But that may be classically memetic, not anomalously memetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect of fear when it rocks is, in fact, memetic by definition because the personnel know about its apparent nature. A meme is information, and a memetic effect is an effect caused by information, which, in this case, is the knowledge that this SCP is or at least resembles a bomb and that bombs are typically designed to explode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like keeping the real creator vague, but I can totally see AWCY? making this, but for a different reason: they might have made the damn thing just to fuck with the Foundation. They're expending personnel and funds on an object that might be completely harmless, but perhaps due to memetics or the fact it COULD be what it says it is, they can't stop. That seems like the right level of douche-y evil from AWCY?, if you think of it in terms of sabotage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was part of the joke, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait. If it's a plastic replica, doesn't that make it a plastic explosive (har har har)?

item.title: Re: Can this be..?
item.content:encoded: It's just as good the second time through.

item.title: Can this be..?
item.content:encoded: Happy +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I signed up here, one of the questions on the questionnaire was, what class would a nuclear bomb be?

As I remember it, the last sentence of my answer was "If it's sentient or something, probably Keter."

This is pretty much what I pictured, except fully-formed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man I am bored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The risk largely outweighs anything the Foundation can do. Even if it's by far unlikely that this isn't a bomb, but it's still risky enough that they can't call its bluff. For more information go watch the Dark Knight Rises.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderfully colorful. Strange and funny.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the only thing it can do to breach containment is go off. It's not exactly going to walk away. And we don't know how it would react to any scans or whatnot. That might just push it over the edge. This thing's Keter because we're forced to rely on its goodwill. If we could do more than keep people away, it wouldn't be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, no attempts to fixate the thing in place, no scans, no actual attempts to contain the thing? Just let it do as it pleases? And to top it off, a Keter?
Nah, doesn't work for me. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author is Faminepulse. He used to be Copernicus, but his account accidentally got deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP. But the author appears to be a deleted account? +1 anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is not an either or situation. If they think SCP-1286 might explode because it looks and acts like a bomb, that is a memetic effect, just a completely natural one.

I came in here to comment about this. Though I suppose it's reasonable enough to assume that the Foundation uses 'memetic effect' as shorthand for 'anomalous memetic effect', the sentence there is still very silly, because the second effect is unambiguously memetic. Just perfectly normal and non-anomalous.

The Foundation should be wondering whether there's an anomalous element to the fear, and whether it's connected to the ordinary memetic effect or is just semi-related Weird Mind Shit, not whether there is a memetic effect at all. So a better way to phrase the sentence would be to replace the word 'memetic' with 'anomalous'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow I missed this and did write it up, and was halfway to posting it before my attention was drawn here.

Damn it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I wasn't that clear with what I meant, I guess. It's good without knowing where it came from, and it doesn't fit well with Are We Cool Yet?'s style, at least to my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how any information about the author would improve this. And well, it's damn good as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, it's not slickly art-school hipster-nihilist menacing enough and too damn likeable to be an Are We Cool Yet? production.

Perhaps a Cold War-era Dadaist precursor to Are We Cool Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had recently thought a suicidal atomic bomb would be a good idea for an SCP. I was going to write it up, but then I saw this.

I really like where you took it. I personally like the ambiguity over whether or not it's an actual clinically depressed bomb or just a sentient sculpture craving attention. It's like if you turned an angsty 13 year old into a nuclear weapon, which is a fucking terrifying concept.

Also, is anyone else reminded of the "ur a faget" comic when they see the face in the picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of have to agree with this. I mean, the ambiguity about whether or not it's actually a bomb is neat, I suppose, but personally I'd at least mention an attempt to determine whether or not it's a bomb that failed due to something or other. Of course, I'm not an expert on detecting the device in a nuclear weapon that'll do the fission/fission and fusion, so I don't know if the Foundation could do it from a safe distance, or if they'd have to get close. That would obviously lead to the rocking, thus the failure if that was the case.

I upvoted in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my thought, along with "There are a few typos" and "What in the hell did I just upvote?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding random links to GoIs does not always improve an article. I like this one a lot just the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there would be more effort put into determining if this thing actually is a bomb or not. On the one hand, it might be an atom bomb with existential angst issues, on the other it might be a harmless sculpture that scares people but has no real damage potential. The containment procedure wouldn't change a whole lot either way, this thing seems to crave the attention and appeasing the quirks of anomalous objects is a big part of what the Foundation does. But don't you think the administration would insist on knowing for sure?

Makes me think of 190, but with a lighter, more humorous touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I deleted the addendum after a few re-reads.

Reason: It was driving a point I had already stressed, and a bit redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of an atom bomb going through an existential crisis is just weird enough to like. Of course, if it is an atom bomb.

I feel like the flow could be improved a bit more, especially from the first paragraph of the description to the second. Maybe just a little bit more text describing how it communicates, or something. I didn't really register that it could talk after the first paragraph, and then the second confused me so I had to go back to reread the first.

This is good enough for me to upvote though. +1

Is anyone else reminded of Marvin the Paranoid Android?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really, maybe. I tried to leave this one up in the air in a lot of different directions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's way too charming to be AWCY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Galleries" is plural. "Gallery's" is possessive.

OnE oF tHeSe ThInGs Is NoT lIkE tHe OtHeR…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I'm not the only one who feels that way. ^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get huge "Are We Cool Yet?" vibes from this. It's great right now but establishing it as possibly related to them would be just fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had some issues with it before the memo, but the memo was the finishing blow for me. This guy is fun but I can't say I like it as a serious scp. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it, but nobody in the whole damn foundation owns a geiger counter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the article, wonderful SCP, but should it be Keter? I mean, we are not even sure that it can explode. For all we know, it could be a guy with a bunch of protein bars and water, shifting his weight whenever someone was close, and, as was said before, this has not been proven to be supernaturally memetic. Could we make this Euclid, or use high-powered X-rays to see if it is even a bomb?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was a little iffy on the concept, but I think It was carried out well, nice job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having flashbacks to Dark Star. Whatever you do, Sanders, don't teach Jeffrey about phenomenology!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I replaced it with "Good."

Without that last box the article has a completely different tone, defeating the concept itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the "Priceless" at the end should be taken out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like the internal memo to stay. Even though you don't like it, what do you think would more effectively imply that they're being toyed with?
